worth_it_explanation: OpenX and the publisher's ad server rarely report the same impression count for the same inventory, and a gap inside a normal range is expected. The audit value is in knowing what that range is and watching it because a discrepancy that drifts wide points at lost revenue, a counting mismatch, or a delivery problem that no single report shows on its own. The check is a read of two reports the publisher already has, so it costs comparison time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points at a concrete number the publisher can defend or chase down.
intro: OpenX, as a sell-side platform (SSP), reports the impressions it sees, and the publisher's own ad server reports the impressions it counts for the same inventory. Those two counts rarely match exactly because each side measures at a different moment in the ad-serving sequence and filters traffic on its own rules. This subcategory is about reconciling the two. These checks confirm that the SSP-versus-ad-server discrepancy is measured against an accepted tolerance rather than left unwatched, and that when the gap moves outside that range, the publisher investigates the cause instead of writing it off.

audit_checks.title: The SSP-versus-ad-server impression discrepancy is not measured against an accepted tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A discrepancy that is never measured cannot be judged, so a publisher has no way to tell a normal gap from one that signals lost revenue or a broken count. The first sign of a billing dispute, a misfiring tag, or a delivery problem often shows up as the discrepancy widening, and a publisher who is not tracking the number misses that signal until it appears as a shortfall.

educate.base:
An impression discrepancy is the difference between two counts of the same inventory. OpenX, as a sell-side platform (SSP), reports the impressions it transacted, and the publisher's ad server reports the impressions it recorded for that same inventory. The two numbers differ as a matter of course because each side counts at a different point in the ad-serving sequence. An ad server may count when it decides to serve an ad or when the ad begins to render, while the SSP counts the impressions it sees clear in its own auction, and the gap between those moments is filled with timeouts, latency, and traffic each side filters differently.

Because some gap is normal, the number that matters is not whether a discrepancy exists but whether it sits inside a range the publisher has accepted. A single-digit-percent gap between an SSP and an ad server is a routine result of the two counting methods. A gap that runs much wider, or one that was narrow last month and is wide this month, points at something worth chasing. Without a measured tolerance, the publisher has no line to compare against and cannot separate the expected gap from the one that signals a problem.

The reconciliation is a read of two reports the publisher already has. The publisher pulls the OpenX impression count and the ad-server impression count for the same inventory over the same date range, then expresses the gap as a percentage of one against the other. That percentage, watched over time, becomes the tolerance. The MRC and IAB Tech Lab revised the impression measurement guidelines to a 'count-on-begin-to-render' minimum, which moved the ad-server counting point later in the sequence and changed the size of the gap publishers should expect, so a tolerance set before that change may no longer fit.

A few signs point to a discrepancy that is not measured against a tolerance:
  • No regular comparison of the OpenX impression count against the ad-server count for the same inventory exists
  • The publisher cannot state the percentage gap they treat as normal, or when it was last checked
  • Reporting is read one platform at a time, so a widening gap between the two is never surfaced
You may want to confirm that the publisher measures the OpenX-versus-ad-server gap as a percentage and holds it against a stated tolerance. One approach is to pull both counts for the same inventory and date range, record the gap, and set the range that counts as normal so a future drift outside it is visible.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, traffic spikes around a breaking story or a newsletter send can push latency up and widen the gap for a short window, so a tolerance set against a quiet baseline may flag normal spikes as problems. You may want to read the discrepancy across both calm and high-traffic periods before settling on the range that counts as normal.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, heavy product pages with multiple scripts can delay the ad call and widen the gap between the SSP count and the ad-server count. You may want to measure the discrepancy on the commercial pages separately from the lighter editorial pages because the two can clear at different gaps and a blended tolerance hides which inventory is drifting.

learn_more.source_title: MRC, IAB Tech Lab, and MMA Announce Revised Ad Measurement Guidelines

investigate.intro: Pull the OpenX impression count and the ad-server impression count for the same inventory over the same date range, express the gap as a percentage, and check whether a tolerance for that gap is on record.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Open the OpenX reporting interface and build an impressions report for the inventory and date range under review.
Record the OpenX impression count for that inventory and date range.
Note the counting method OpenX applies so it can be compared against the ad server's method.

ad_server_reporting.label: Publisher ad server reporting
steps: Open the publisher's ad server and build a matching impressions report for the same inventory and the same date range.
Record the ad-server impression count and express the gap against the OpenX count as a percentage.
Ask the publisher what percentage gap they treat as normal and when it was last reviewed, and record whether a tolerance is documented or absent.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the OpenX impression count and the ad-server impression count for the same inventory and date range, the gap between them as a percentage, and whether a stated tolerance for that gap exists. A setup where the discrepancy is measured regularly and held against a documented tolerance passes. A setup where no comparison is run, or where the publisher cannot state the gap they treat as normal, is a fail recorded with the two counts and the unmeasured gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the OpenX and ad-server impression counts for the same inventory, the percentage gap, and whether a tolerance exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not measure the impression discrepancy between OpenX and the ad server against a stated tolerance, so a normal gap cannot be told apart from one that signals lost revenue or a broken count. The two platforms count at different points in the ad-serving sequence, so some gap is expected, but without a measured percentage held against a range, a widening discrepancy goes unseen until it shows up as a shortfall. {{voice_recommend}} pulling the OpenX impression count and the ad-server count for the same inventory and date range, expressing the gap as a percentage, and setting the range that counts as normal so a future drift outside it is visible. After the tolerance is set, recheck the gap on a regular schedule rather than one platform at a time.

audit_checks.title: Discrepancy causes are not investigated when the gap runs outside tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A discrepancy that runs wide and is left unexamined hides its own cause, which may be a header-bidding timeout dropping bids, latency on heavy pages, a counting-method mismatch, or creatives the ad server blocked. Each cause points at a different fix, and revenue keeps leaking on the timeout and latency cases for as long as the gap goes unexplained.

educate.base:
When the gap between the OpenX count and the ad-server count runs outside the accepted tolerance, the next step is to find the cause. A wide discrepancy is a symptom rather than a diagnosis. Several causes produce the same widening gap, and each one points at a different fix, so the publisher gains nothing from knowing the gap is wide until they know why.

The common causes fall into a few groups:
  • Header-bidding timeouts: a bid that arrives after the auction timeout is discarded, so OpenX may record activity the ad server never counts as a served impression
  • Latency: a slow page or a heavy script delays the ad call, which widens the window where the two counting points diverge
  • Counting method: one side counts earlier in the sequence than the other, for example an ad server counting on begin-to-render against an SSP counting its cleared auctions
  • Blocked or failed creatives: a creative the ad server blocks, or one that fails to render, is counted on one side but not the other
Prebid's own guidance on analyzing a header-bidding setup treats timeouts and bidder latency as the first things to read when impressions do not reconcile. A timeout set too tight drops bids that would otherwise have cleared, and a timeout set too loose delays the ad call and invites the latency gap. Reading the timeout configuration and the latency profile narrows a wide discrepancy toward a specific cause.

The investigation is a process of elimination rather than a single lookup. The publisher checks the auction timeout against bidder response times, reads page latency on the inventory where the gap is widest, confirms the two platforms are counting at comparable points, and reviews whether blocked or failed creatives account for part of the gap. Each check either explains a slice of the discrepancy or rules a cause out.

You may want to confirm that a discrepancy outside tolerance triggers an investigation into its cause rather than being written off. One approach is to work through the common causes in order, starting with the timeout and latency on the inventory where the gap is widest, and record which cause each slice of the gap traces back to.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the inventory where the gap is widest is often the heaviest editorial pages or the units furthest down a long article, where latency stacks up and bids miss the timeout. You may want to start the investigation on those pages rather than on the homepage because the homepage usually loads fast and reconciles cleanly while the deep pages carry the discrepancy.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages run more scripts than editorial pages, so latency and timeout-related drops concentrate there. You may want to read the timeout configuration and page latency on the commercial templates first because that is where a wide gap most often traces back to a bid that arrived after the auction closed.

investigate.intro: On the inventory where the gap runs widest, work through the common causes in order, reading the auction timeout against bidder response times, page latency, the counting method on each side, and any blocked or failed creatives.

prebid_config_review.label: Header bidding configuration review
steps: Read the Prebid auction timeout and the failsafe timeout in the wrapper configuration, and compare the auction timeout against the typical bidder response times.
Note any bidders whose responses regularly arrive after the timeout because those bids are discarded and can widen the OpenX-versus-ad-server gap.
Record whether the timeout is set tight enough to drop bids that would otherwise clear, or loose enough to delay the ad call and add latency.

latency_and_counting_review.label: Latency and counting-method review
steps: Measure page latency on the inventory where the discrepancy is widest, using browser developer tools to time the ad call from page load.
Confirm the counting point each platform uses, for example whether the ad server counts on begin-to-render while OpenX counts its cleared auctions, and note any mismatch.
Review whether blocked or failed creatives appear on the affected inventory, and record which cause each slice of the gap traces back to.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for a discrepancy that ran outside tolerance, whether an investigation was carried out and what cause each slice of the gap traced back to across timeouts, latency, counting method, and blocked creatives. A setup where an out-of-range gap triggered an investigation that named the causes passes. A setup where a wide discrepancy was written off without examining timeout, latency, counting, or creative causes is a fail recorded with the unexplained gap and the inventory it affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the out-of-tolerance gap and the cause each slice traces back to across timeout, latency, counting method, and creatives above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} leaves a wide impression discrepancy between OpenX and the ad server unexamined, so its cause is unknown. A gap outside tolerance is a symptom that can trace back to a header-bidding timeout dropping bids, latency on heavy pages, a counting-method mismatch, or blocked creatives, and each cause points at a different fix while revenue keeps leaking on the timeout and latency cases. {{voice_recommend}} working through the common causes in order on the inventory where the gap is widest, starting with the auction timeout against bidder response times and page latency, then confirming the counting method on each side and reviewing blocked creatives. After each slice of the gap is traced to a cause, address the causes that point at lost impressions first.
